sources.notes: Statement and constraints: compute a single term a_l of an infinite binary sequence defined by prefix XOR, with l up to 1e18.
Official editorial precomputes up to 2n terms, makes n odd, then uses a halving recurrence for indices beyond the precomputed region.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Given the first n bits of an infinite sequence where later terms are prefix XORs, answer the value of one huge-index term.
annotation.constraints: n totals 2e5, but l may be 1e18, so only O(n) preprocessing plus logarithmic recursion in the queried index is viable.
annotation.core_idea: Precompute enough initial terms and, if needed, append one term so n is odd. For large indices, terms come in equal pairs and the prefix-XOR definition reduces a_x to either the total prefix XOR p or p xor a_{floor(x/2)}. Thus the query index can be repeatedly halved until it falls into the precomputed range.
annotation.complexity: O(n + log l) per test case, O(n) memory.
tricks: Normalize n to odd so pairs beyond n cancel cleanly in prefix XOR.
Derive a self-similar halving recurrence for huge indices.
Precompute the first 2n terms to provide a recursion base.

solution_variants.name: prefix-xor-recursive-halving
solution_variants.summary: Build prefix sums/XORs and terms up to 2n. For x beyond that range, apply the parity-dependent formula that either returns p or recurses to floor(x/2), accumulating XOR contributions.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n + log l) time, O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The query is solved by repeatedly reducing a huge index to floor(x/2) until reaching the precomputed prefix.
tags.description: Reduce a huge index or state by repeatedly halving it, using a derived self-similar recurrence until it reaches a small precomputed base case.
tags.created_reason: Existing recursive-construction tags cover building objects, while this problem needs retrieval for huge-index evaluation by recursive halving.

tags.evidence: The main step is deriving the prefix-XOR identity that collapses a_x to p or p xor a_{floor(x/2)}.
The sequence is binary and all transitions are based on XOR parity.
The solution uses pair cancellation/equal neighboring terms, but not a full periodic-cycle argument.
